# May 13, 2026

*at \~9 PM, PST*



<br>



After much time off, I have finally made *an addition to the website*.

## HOLY FUCKING SHIT

I know, I know. Pretty exciting, right?



<br>



You might be asking "but what is Shadez 2?". If you asked 9 year old me, I'd have told you that it is a game I suck ass at (except I didn't cuss when I was 9). However, I can tell you with sincerity that it is quite a nostalgic and surprisingly competent 2D real-time strategy flash game made by Sean Cooper and set in an alternate reality where we have made first contact with a hostile alien species, and are fighting for our existence.



Highly original, I know.



At any rate, I can confidently say that it is one of my favorite flash games ever, and was therefore worth immortalizing on my own website.



<br>



# So that's it?



Not even close! I have made a few subtle changes to the game using a cool piece of open-source software called JPEXS Free Flash Decompiler. To explain, I must first tell you about the microtransactions this game shipped with. Shadez 2 integrated with a now-defunct company known as MochiMedia, who stored user data, shop items, purchase history, etc. and provided an ActionScript 3 API for developers to embed in their games. That last part is important, because this API can be inspected and... *massaged* using the aforementioned decompiler.



With that said, I'd like to introduce you to a function called **StoreItemPurchased**.



<img src="data:image/webp;base64," style="width: 40vw;"/>



This function resides inside the **Local.Comms.MochiMedia.CMochi** class and normally contains a fair bit more code. However, simply changing it to return true causes the game to believe that you have purchased everything. Does this seem too good to be true? Are you expecting some kind of twist? *Reality check?* There isn't one, it really is that simple.



With that said, I also went about embedding the Rust-based Ruffle flash emulator and my "cracked" Shadez 2 .swf into a new page, currently accessible via the Navbar. I also went insane for about 2 hours trying to figure out how to scale-up Ruffle so that the viewport wasn't utterly tiny. After doing that, it's big now. You're welcome.



Ideally, this will live in some kind of gallery where I can show off other such projects in the future, and preferably with a pretty UI as well. For now, however, it will stand proud in its current location as a token of 9-year-old me's gaming history. Now, you all can enjoy as well :)
